The Asus Instant Guard app makes setup easy as well.

One thing that sets Asus apart is its software. It not only allows access to the router’s settings using an app or web browser but also comes with free parental control software thanks to AiProtection powered by Trend Micro. You can set up profiles for each member of the family with specific blocks in place including ad-blocking. Overall, this router is a strong pick for most families looking for speed support up to 1Gbps.

TP-Link Archer AX55
Best value

Plenty of speed for most without going overboard

The Archer AX55 is an affordable AX3000 Wi-Fi 6 router with 160MHz support and four gigabit Ethernet ports. This router has enough power to keep up with a gigabit internet connection with multiple devices at once.

Pros
  • Plenty of speed for gigabit connections
  • Mesh support with EasyMesh
  • Easy setup with the TP-Link Tether app
Cons
  • Full security and parental controls cost extra
  • Coverage could be better
$108 at Amazon

Most people shopping for a router aren’t interested in multi-gig speeds with advanced gaming features, and really, just want enough speed for the family to stream, game, and browse without any issues. The TP-Link Archer isn’t the cheapest router you can buy with its AX3000 connection, but it’s a good choice for most people and should last a few years before needing to be replaced. The AX3000 connection works out to 574Mbps at 2.4GHz and 2402Mbps at 5GHz with 160MHZ support. That means that a supported device can connect at up to 2402Mbps, though most Wi-Fi 6 devices will only support 1201Mbps connections.

On the back of the router, there are four gigabit Ethernet ports for your wired devices, with another port for the incoming connection. There’s a USB 3.0 port on the back that supports common Windows and Mac formats like NTFS and HFS+ so you can easily access it from your PC. You can even pick up one of the best external hard drives for Mac to use with the Time Machine backup feature.

On the software front, TP-Link has included its HomeShield security and parental control features. While some features require a paid subscription, most people will be happy with the free options. TP-Link also includes EasyMesh support on all hardware revisions of this router, so you can add it as a mesh node. If you end up with versions 1 or 1.6, though, you’ll need to apply a software update to get the mesh features.

ASUS RT-AX88U Pro
Best gaming router

Get the best gaming speeds without wasting money on RGB

$240 $300 Save $60

The Asus RT-AX88U Pro looks like a fairly standard AX6000 router at first, but with a fast CPU and multi-gig Ethernet ports, this router is one of the best you can get for gaming. You can prioritize your gaming consoles and PC in software to keep pings low and consistent, even when others in the house are using the internet.

Pros
  • Dual 2.5Gbps Ethernet ports
  • AiProtection Pro is included for free
  • VPN Fusion, OpenNAT, and AiMesh are supported
Cons
  • Only dual-band Wi-Fi
  • Fairly expensive for AX6000
  • No RGB lights
$240 at Amazon $240 at Best Buy $270 at Asus

If a router can't keep up with all the traffic that it needs to route, it's going to lead to latency spikes, which will cause lag in games. A good gaming router will have plenty of power to keep up with all of your network traffic without slowing down, and can even prioritize your own gaming traffic if usage is high. The Asus RT-AX88U Pro is an AX6000 Wi-Fi 6 router with a powerful CPU and robust software with a QoS for gaming. The connection speed breaks down to 574Mbps on the 2.4GHz band and 4,804Mbps on the 5GHz band. With 4x4 MIMO, this router should be able to keep up with the needs of a family with ease.

Around the back, you get a USB 3.0 port, two 2.5Gbps Ethernet ports, and four more Ethernet ports running at 1Gbps. One of the 2.5Gbps ports can be used for your incoming connection, meaning you're ready for multi-gig internet speeds that are reaching a few ISPs. You can also connect a fast wired device to it, such as a gaming PC or a NAS, which can be perfect for streamers who want to save a high-quality VOD without eating up their local storage space.

On the software side, the ASUS RT has most of the features gamers are looking for, with a QoS allowing you to categorize and prioritize your devices, so the most important ones always have the speed they need. Furthermore, a feature called OpenNAT makes it easy to create a rule for a game server if you're experiencing strict or double NAT. There's even a mobile gaming mode in the Asus Router app that, with a tap, will optimize your phone's connection for gaming, which can be essential for top performance in competitive mobile games.

TP-Link Archer Air R5
Best wall-mounted router

Optimal positioning without getting in the way

If you’re looking for a low-profile router that stays out of the way, the Archer Air R5 is a strong choice that fits right against your wall with included mounts. It’s even got plenty of speed for a family with an AX3000 Wi-Fi 6 connection.

Pros
  • Ultra-thin design for wall-mounting
  • Quick AX3000 connection with 160MHz support
  • EasyMesh expansion supported
Cons
  • Only one Ethernet LAN port
  • Some HomeShield features require a subscription
$120 at Amazon

Apart from some new materials, RGB lighting, and more advanced antennas, most Wi-Fi routers look like the ancient WRT-54G that I had used to play Mario Kart DS back in 2005. As Wi-Fi has become more important in our lives, it makes sense that router design would allow us to put routers out in the open without taking up shelf space. The TP-Link Archer Air R5 is an AX3000 router with dual Ethernet ports that fits flush against your wall and supports mesh expansion.

The Archer Air R5 is less than an inch deep and can be stuck straight to the wall using the included tape, or with a separate plastic mounting bracket. It’s powered by a single USB-C port on the bottom between the two Ethernet ports, so there are no wires hanging out the top like they do with a traditional router design. The antennas are also integrated around the edge, so with the matte white coloring, you might even forget you have this installed in your living room.

If you want to expand your network with a mesh, you can use other EasyMesh routers as nodes, including more Air R5s, but there’s also a matching Air E5 extender that has the same aesthetic and no Ethernet ports. There’s a lot to like about these ultra-thin routers, including the price, which is just over $120. If you can get by with only one Ethernet port, this is a great low-profile option.

Eero Max 7
Best Wi-Fi 7 router

Speed and simplicity

$510 $600 Save $90

While not the fastest Wi-Fi 7 router, the Eero Max 7 is a great way to get multi-gig wireless and wired speeds all over your home. It also supports mesh expansion with a wide range of other Eero nodes.

Pros
  • Fast BE20800 tri-band connection with 10 spatial streams
  • Two 10Gbps Ethernet ports per node
  • Sleek fan-less design
Cons
  • No access to advanced settings
  • Eero Secure costs extra
  • An Eero account is required
$510 at Amazon $510 at Best Buy $510 at Eero

If you’re looking for a basic but fast Wi-Fi solution, Eero is one of the first brands you should consider. While a lot of Eero’s older routers were a bit of a letdown in the hardware department, the Eero Max 7 starts off on the right foot with a powerful BE20800 tri-band Wi-Fi 7 connection. It supports speeds up to 573.5Mbps at 2.4GHz, 8647.1Mbps at 5Ghz, and 11529.4Mbps at 6GHz with 4x4 streams at both 5GHz and 6GHz. On the back, Eero has equipped the Max 7 with two 2.5Gbps and two 10Gbps Ethernet ports that can be used for both WAN and LAN.

While you can buy a single Eero router, and it will work great, the product was really designed with mesh in mind. With a second Eero router, you can extend your coverage, especially 6GHz coverage, without giving up much speed. Eero uses its own proprietary TrueMesh technology to link the nodes which can use both 5GHz and 6GHz depending on which gives the better connection. One nice thing about Eero is that it supports all older Eero nodes for expansion, so if you’ve got lower priority areas, you can save with a cheaper node.

Read our review
Eero Max 7 review: Eero's simplicity elevates Wi-Fi 7's appeal
Eero Max 7 is a powerful Wi-fi 7 mesh system with more speed than most people can use and multi-gig network support.

You must use the Eero app for setup and management, which also means you’ll need an Eero account. If you prefer to connect to your router using a web browser for advanced tools, Eero is not the solution for you. Still, with strong performance and a simple setup process, a lot of people will be very happy with Eero’s software (and the control app). There is an Eero Secure subscription available with some security software like a VPN, malware, and antivirus protection, as well as VIP support, but it requires a separate monthly or yearly subscription.

TP-Link Archer BE550
Best value Wi-Fi 7

Wi-Fi 7 support for Wi-Fi 6E prices

Wi-Fi 7 is here, and so are the value routers starting with the BE9300 Archer BE550. This tri-band router supports 320MHz 6GHz Wi-Fi speeds with 2.5Gbps Ethernet for all wired devices.

Pros
  • Plenty of speed for most residential internet connections
  • Fast speeds at both 5GHz and 6GHz
  • All Ethernet ports are 2.5Gbps
Cons
  • Not the fastest possible 5GHz setup
  • No display on the front
  • Full HomeShield features cost extra
$300 at Amazon $300 at B&H $300 at Newegg

Make no mistake, there are Wi-Fi 6 and Wi-Fi 6E routers with higher combined speeds than this router, but those routers are constrained by their use of Wi-Fi 6 technology and 160MHz bands. The TP-Link Archer BE550 is a BE9300 router with 574Mbps at 2.4GHz thanks to using Wi-Fi 6, 2880Mbps at 5GHz with 160MHz support, and 5760Mbps at 6GHz with a 320MHz band. On the back, there’s a single 2.5Gbps Ethernet port for the incoming connection with four 2.5Gbps ports for LAN devices, including 5Gbps support with LAN aggregation using two ports.

For most of your older tech using Wi-Fi 5 or Wi-Fi 6, you won’t see much of a speed boost over an AX3000 router, but if you’ve got tech that supports 6GHz Wi-Fi, you could see some immediate speed improvements. Not to mention the fact that you’ll be clearing up capacity at 5GHz by moving some devices to 6GHz.

EasyMesh support is included with most of TP-Link's Wi-Fi 7 routers, so you can use this router in an EasyMesh setup. TP-Link includes its HomeShield software for improved security and parental controls. This software has a few nice free features to get you by, but to get all the security and parental control options, you’ll need to sign up for a paid subscription.

GL.iNet GL-MT3000
Wi-Fi that travels with you

Connect once and use hotel Wi-Fi on all of your devices

$81 $99 Save $18

This compact AX3000 Wi-Fi 6 router is designed to make travel better, especially when you have no choice but to use hotel Wi-Fi. This router allows you to log in to hotel Wi-Fi and share that connection with your other devices, so you don't need to connect each one individually. With support for VPNs, you can also maintain your privacy without giving up up much, if any, speed.

Pros
  • Fast AX3000 connection supports multiple devices
  • USB-C power makes getting connected in other countries easy
  • OpenWRT operating system supports a ton of features
Cons
  • Coverage isn't sufficient for the whole house
  • Only one Ethernet port
$84 at Amazon $81 at Walmart $84 at Gl.iNet

The GL-MT3000 Beryl AX router from GL.iNet is an impressive little router from any angle. It features a quick AX3000 dual-band Wi-Fi 6 wireless connection with a 2.5Gbps Ethernet port. The 2.4GHz wireless band supports speeds up to 574Mbps with up to 2402Mbps at 5GHz. It can even use DFS bands in the United States, United Kingdom, Europe, and Japan if you travel abroad. With two small external antennas and an internal antenna, coverage isn't amazing, but it should be fine for a small apartment or hotel room.

This router is small enough to fit in one hand, so it's easy to pack, and with power via USB-C, it's easy to get a compatible power adapter in any country. If you're in a hotel, the router can connect using Ethernet if a wired connection is available, but can also connect to the hotel Wi-Fi with a browser that can get past captive login pages that require you to accept terms or enter a password. Thanks to this, you won't need to go through the access process on every device you want to get online.

The GL-MT3000 runs OpenWRT, a powerful and in-depth operating system for routers which allows for VPN connections, local network storage, ad-blocking, and much more. While this software could be a bit overwhelming for new users, it's very powerful for those willing to learn it. Compared to some of it's older routers, Gl.iNet has even gotten VPN speeds as high as 300Mbps with Wireguard and 150Mbps with OpenVPN for those that want to connect through a VPN.

Netgear Nighthawk M6
Best 5G router

Works with AT&T or T-Mobile

$650 $700 Save $50

If you’re on the move a lot in an RV, boat, or truck, a strong portable 5G router can give you better network performance and speeds than using the hotspot on your phone. The Netgear Nighthawk M6 MR6150 is an unlocked hotspot with Wi-Fi 6 speeds, external antenna support, and a mode to supply internet to a home router or mesh.

Pros
  • Support for fast mid-band 5G connection
  • Not locked to a specific carrier
  • Solid AX3600 Wi-Fi 6 connection for up to 32 clients
Cons
  • No mmWave 5G support
  • Quite expensive
$650 at Amazon $650 at Best Buy $650 at Netgear

Netgear’s 5G hotspots are really in a league of their own with excellent design and easy expandability. The Nighthawk M6 hotspot supports a wide range of 5G and LTE bands, including the latest mid-band coverage on AT&T and T-Mobile. Support for up to 6x carrier aggregation LTE + 5GNR allows you to make the most of the available connection. This model is unlocked, so you’ll need to purchase a data plan for it on your own, but you’re also not tied to a specific carrier.

It’s also compatible with TS-9 antennas, so you can get the best signal possible in an RV. This can also be a great solution for those who simply need to work far away from a fixed internet connection, especially when you remember that 5G Home Internet from carriers like T-Mobile is only supposed to be used in a single location.

The Nighthawk M6 has a wireless connection that tops out at Wi-Fi 6 AX3600 with 2900Mbps at 5GHz and 700Mbps at 2.4GHz. This is good for up to 32 connected devices at once. There’s a gigabit Ethernet port on the bottom, so you can connect a device directly. This Ethernet port also supports connecting to another router, so if you want to use your 5G internet at home, you can simply plug it in. It even has an operating mode specifically for this, which disables onboard Wi-Fi and the battery.

Get fast internet across your whole home with the best Wi-Fi routers

Asus offers one of the most complete packages of any router, with its free security and parental control software, high-end hardware, and strong mesh support between models. The RT-AX5400 is a fairly standard router, but with dual-band Wi-Fi 6 speeds, including 160MHz support at 5GHz, it's got plenty of speed for even a large family with an appetite for streaming. The included parental controls allow you to block certain types of websites as well as ads, so you can take back control of what your kids see online.

The RT-AX5400 is a solid router with few bells and whistles, but with a low price that makes it a good buy for just about any family. The fact that it may be more powerful than many people need is just icing on the cake and could be handy as internet speeds continue to improve.

Asus RT-AX5400
Editor's choice

The Asus RT-AX5400 is a quick router with plenty of power for large families and gamers alike. This is a great router for internet connections for internet speeds up to 1000Mbps.

$180 at Amazon $180 at Walmart $160 at B&H

If you're a gamer, however, your needs are a bit different. Gaming has more to do with latency than raw speed, with even the best gaming monitors or the best gaming mice getting response times down to just 1 ms in some cases. All the intention here is to keep the action on your screen feeling as immediate as possible. With online gaming, the responsiveness of your game is dependent on your latency to the server and the quality of your connection. Gaming routers can't pick a faster connection to the server - that's up to your ISP and the laws of physics. But the right router can make sure your gaming traffic reaches your ISP as soon as possible and ahead of less important traffic on your network.

Prioritizing traffic means that if someone in your home starts streaming a Netflix show, your gaming data is delivered consistently so that it doesn't get stuck behind a chunk of buffering video. It doesn't really matter if a movie has to wait in line for a few milliseconds, but the difference can be much more profound in a real-time application like an online game. And if you enjoy game streaming from a Chromebook, like the Acer Chromebook 516 GE, every millisecond counts. The ASUS RT-AX88U Pro has plenty of wireless capacity to handle a family of gamers thanks to its AX6000 dual-band connection. And if you're a content creator, the 2.5Gbps Ethernet port is perfect for saving high-quality VODs to a fast NAS from a streaming PC, offering the speed to stream in full quality at the same time.

If you're considering upgrading to Wi-Fi 6E, there are a few things you know before hitting the buy button. The first thing is that Wi-Fi 6E is mostly the same as Wi-Fi 6 plus access to the 6GHz spectrum, which, since it's new, has very little congestion. If you're trying to get as much speed as possible in an apartment building or condominium, this can be extremely helpful; but in a suburb or rural neighborhood, the 6 to 6E upgrade would not be worthwhile as you may see no uplift with 6GHz at all. Wi-Fi 7 routers are also becoming available, like the TP-Link Archer BE800, and can make better use of the 6GHz spectrum with wider 320MHz bands.
item.content:encoded:
Wi-Fi has become the center of our connected lives, whether we have just a few devices or a house stuffed with smart home tech. While competition and necessity have pushed internet service providers to increase their internet speeds, many people will need a newer router and modem to take advantage of this increase. ISP-provided gateways can be plenty fast, yet they remain a one-size-fits-all Wi-Fi solution, and have a more limited feature set than a router from another company could offer.

If you're looking to get the best speeds possible in a suburban home, or make the most of your network in a congested apartment building, upgrading your home network with the right router has never been easier. Ranging from expensive to affordable, here are the best ones available.

  • Source: Asus
    Asus RT-AX5400
    Best overall
    $160 at B&H
  • TP-Link Archer AX55
    Best value
    $108 at Amazon
  • Source: ASUS
    ASUS RT-AX88U Pro
    Best gaming router
    $240 at Amazon
  • Source: TP-Link
    TP-Link Archer Air R5
    Best wall-mounted router
    $120 at Amazon
  • Source: Amazon
    Eero Max 7
    Best Wi-Fi 7 router
    $510 at Amazon
  • Source: TP-Link
    TP-Link Archer BE550
    Best value Wi-Fi 7
    $300 at Amazon
  • Source: GL.iNet
    GL.iNet GL-MT3000
    Wi-Fi that travels with you
    $81 at Walmart
  • Source: Netgear
    Netgear Nighthawk M6
    Best 5G router
    $650 at Amazon

These are the best Wi-Fi routers to buy in 2024

Asus RT-AX5400
Best overall

Excellent speeds for the price

For most families, the Asus RT-AX5400 has plenty of power to keep up with a gigabit internet speed with plenty of capacity for multiple 4K streams, gaming, and working from home.

Pros
  • Plenty of speed for even large families
  • Easy Mesh expansion with AiMesh
  • Free parental controls with AiProtection
Cons
  • No multi-gig WAN support
  • Must agree to share data with Trend Micro for AiProtection features
$180 at Amazon $180 at Walmart $160 at B&H

Asus routers can get a little out of hand, with high-end models overdoing it with RGB and features that most people simply don’t need. The Asus RT-AX5400, on the other hand, is a middle-of-the-road dual-band Wi-Fi 6 router, with no frills, which makes it a great pick for a lot of people. The dual-band Wi-Fi 6 connection on this router has 574Mbps available at 2.4GHz, and 4802Mbps at 5GHz. Wireless speeds are kept high with 160MHz support, 1024-QAM, and OFDMA.

The RT-AX5400 is designed for gigabit internet speeds with gigabit Ethernet for WAN, and four gigabit Ethernet ports of LAN. There’s also a USB-A 3.2 port for network storage or even WAN from an LTE or 5G device. If you need more coverage, AiMesh expansion is supported so you can build a mesh network using another Asus router as a node. Want to connect to your home network with a VPN while away? The Asus Instant Guard app makes setup easy as well.

One thing that sets Asus apart is its software. It not only allows access to the router’s settings using an app or web browser but also comes with free parental control software thanks to AiProtection powered by Trend Micro. You can set up profiles for each member of the family with specific blocks in place including ad-blocking. Overall, this router is a strong pick for most families looking for speed support up to 1Gbps.

TP-Link Archer AX55
Best value

Plenty of speed for most without going overboard

The Archer AX55 is an affordable AX3000 Wi-Fi 6 router with 160MHz support and four gigabit Ethernet ports. This router has enough power to keep up with a gigabit internet connection with multiple devices at once.

Pros
  • Plenty of speed for gigabit connections
  • Mesh support with EasyMesh
  • Easy setup with the TP-Link Tether app
Cons
  • Full security and parental controls cost extra
  • Coverage could be better
$108 at Amazon

TP-Link also includes EasyMesh support on all hardware revisions of this router, so you can add it as a mesh node. If you end up with versions 1 or 1.6, though, you’ll need to apply a software update to get the mesh features.

ASUS RT-AX88U Pro
Best gaming router

Get the best gaming speeds without wasting money on RGB

$240 $300 Save $60

Cons

  • Fairly expensive for AX6000
  • No RGB lights

  • Ultra-thin design for wall-mounting
  • Quick AX3000 connection with 160MHz support
  • EasyMesh expansion supported
Cons
  • Only one Ethernet LAN port
  • Some HomeShield features require a subscription
$120 at Amazon

Eero Max 7
Best Wi-Fi 7 router

Speed and simplicity

$510 $600 Save $90

Pros
  • Fast BE20800 tri-band connection with 10 spatial streams
  • Two 10Gbps Ethernet ports per node
  • Sleek fan-less design
Cons
  • No access to advanced settings
  • Eero Secure costs extra
  • An Eero account is required
$510 at Amazon $510 at Best Buy $510 at Eero

Eero Max 7 review: Eero's simplicity elevates Wi-Fi 7's appeal
Eero Max 7 is a powerful Wi-fi 7 mesh system with more speed than most people can use and multi-gig network support.

TP-Link Archer BE550
Best value Wi-Fi 7

Wi-Fi 7 support for Wi-Fi 6E prices

  • Fast speeds at both 5GHz and 6GHz
  • All Ethernet ports are 2.5Gbps
Cons
  • Not the fastest possible 5GHz setup
  • No display on the front
  • Full HomeShield features cost extra
$300 at Amazon $300 at B&H $300 at Newegg

Make no mistake, there are Wi-Fi 6 and Wi-Fi 6E routers with higher combined speeds than this router, but those routers are constrained by their use of Wi-Fi 6 technology and 160MHz bands. The TP-Link Archer BE550 is a BE9300 router with 574Mbps at 2.4GHz thanks to using Wi-Fi 6, 2880Mbps at 5GHz with 160MHz support, and 5760Mbps at 6GHz with a 320MHz band. On the back, there’s a single 2.5Gbps Ethernet port for the incoming connection with four 2.5Gbps ports for LAN devices, including 5Gbps support with LAN aggregation using two ports.

For most of your older tech using Wi-Fi 5 or Wi-Fi 6, you won’t see much of a speed boost over an AX3000 router, but if you’ve got tech that supports 6GHz Wi-Fi, you could see some immediate speed improvements. Not to mention the fact that you’ll be clearing up capacity at 5GHz by moving some devices to 6GHz.

EasyMesh support is included with most of TP-Link's Wi-Fi 7 routers, so you can use this router in an EasyMesh setup. TP-Link includes its HomeShield software for improved security and parental controls. This software has a few nice free features to get you by, but to get all the security and parental control options, you’ll need to sign up for a paid subscription.

GL.iNet GL-MT3000
Wi-Fi that travels with you

Connect once and use hotel Wi-Fi on all of your devices

$81 $99 Save $18

This compact AX3000 Wi-Fi 6 router is designed to make travel better, especially when you have no choice but to use hotel Wi-Fi. This router allows you to log in to hotel Wi-Fi and share that connection with your other devices, so you don't need to connect each one individually. With support for VPNs, you can also maintain your privacy without giving up up much, if any, speed.

Pros
  • Fast AX3000 connection supports multiple devices
  • USB-C power makes getting connected in other countries easy
  • OpenWRT operating system supports a ton of features
Cons
  • Coverage isn't sufficient for the whole house
  • Only one Ethernet port
$84 at Amazon $81 at Walmart $84 at Gl.iNet

The GL-MT3000 Beryl AX router from GL.iNet is an impressive little router from any angle. It features a quick AX3000 dual-band Wi-Fi 6 wireless connection with a 2.5Gbps Ethernet port. The 2.4GHz wireless band supports speeds up to 574Mbps with up to 2402Mbps at 5GHz. It can even use DFS bands in the United States, United Kingdom, Europe, and Japan if you travel abroad. With two small external antennas and an internal antenna, coverage isn't amazing, but it should be fine for a small apartment or hotel room.

This router is small enough to fit in one hand, so it's easy to pack, and with power via USB-C, it's easy to get a compatible power adapter in any country. If you're in a hotel, the router can connect using Ethernet if a wired connection is available, but can also connect to the hotel Wi-Fi with a browser that can get past captive login pages that require you to accept terms or enter a password. Thanks to this, you won't need to go through the access process on every device you want to get online.

The GL-MT3000 runs OpenWRT, a powerful and in-depth operating system for routers which allows for VPN connections, local network storage, ad-blocking, and much more. While this software could be a bit overwhelming for new users, it's very powerful for those willing to learn it. Compared to some of it's older routers, Gl.iNet has even gotten VPN speeds as high as 300Mbps with Wireguard and 150Mbps with OpenVPN for those that want to connect through a VPN.

Netgear Nighthawk M6
Best 5G router

Works with AT&T or T-Mobile

$650 $700 Save $50

If you’re on the move a lot in an RV, boat, or truck, a strong portable 5G router can give you better network performance and speeds than using the hotspot on your phone. The Netgear Nighthawk M6 MR6150 is an unlocked hotspot with Wi-Fi 6 speeds, external antenna support, and a mode to supply internet to a home router or mesh.

Pros
  • Support for fast mid-band 5G connection
  • Not locked to a specific carrier
  • Solid AX3600 Wi-Fi 6 connection for up to 32 clients
Cons
  • No mmWave 5G support
  • Quite expensive
$650 at Amazon $650 at Best Buy $650 at Netgear

Netgear’s 5G hotspots are really in a league of their own with excellent design and easy expandability. The Nighthawk M6 hotspot supports a wide range of 5G and LTE bands, including the latest mid-band coverage on AT&T and T-Mobile. Support for up to 6x carrier aggregation LTE + 5GNR allows you to make the most of the available connection. This model is unlocked, so you’ll need to purchase a data plan for it on your own, but you’re also not tied to a specific carrier.

It’s also compatible with TS-9 antennas, so you can get the best signal possible in an RV. This can also be a great solution for those who simply need to work far away from a fixed internet connection, especially when you remember that 5G Home Internet from carriers like T-Mobile is only supposed to be used in a single location.

The Nighthawk M6 has a wireless connection that tops out at Wi-Fi 6 AX3600 with 2900Mbps at 5GHz and 700Mbps at 2.4GHz. This is good for up to 32 connected devices at once. There’s a gigabit Ethernet port on the bottom, so you can connect a device directly. This Ethernet port also supports connecting to another router, so if you want to use your 5G internet at home, you can simply plug it in. It even has an operating mode specifically for this, which disables onboard Wi-Fi and the battery.

Get fast internet across your whole home with the best Wi-Fi routers

Asus offers one of the most complete packages of any router, with its free security and parental control software, high-end hardware, and strong mesh support between models. The RT-AX5400 is a fairly standard router, but with dual-band Wi-Fi 6 speeds, including 160MHz support at 5GHz, it's got plenty of speed for even a large family with an appetite for streaming. The included parental controls allow you to block certain types of websites as well as ads, so you can take back control of what your kids see online.

The RT-AX5400 is a solid router with few bells and whistles, but with a low price that makes it a good buy for just about any family. The fact that it may be more powerful than many people need is just icing on the cake and could be handy as internet speeds continue to improve.

Asus RT-AX5400
Editor's choice

The Asus RT-AX5400 is a quick router with plenty of power for large families and gamers alike. This is a great router for internet connections for internet speeds up to 1000Mbps.

$180 at Amazon $180 at Walmart $160 at B&H

If you're a gamer, however, your needs are a bit different. Gaming has more to do with latency than raw speed, with even the best gaming monitors or the best gaming mice getting response times down to just 1 ms in some cases. All the intention here is to keep the action on your screen feeling as immediate as possible. With online gaming, the responsiveness of your game is dependent on your latency to the server and the quality of your connection. Gaming routers can't pick a faster connection to the server - that's up to your ISP and the laws of physics. But the right router can make sure your gaming traffic reaches your ISP as soon as possible and ahead of less important traffic on your network.

Prioritizing traffic means that if someone in your home starts streaming a Netflix show, your gaming data is delivered consistently so that it doesn't get stuck behind a chunk of buffering video. It doesn't really matter if a movie has to wait in line for a few milliseconds, but the difference can be much more profound in a real-time application like an online game. And if you enjoy game streaming from a Chromebook, like the Acer Chromebook 516 GE, every millisecond counts. The ASUS RT-AX88U Pro has plenty of wireless capacity to handle a family of gamers thanks to its AX6000 dual-band connection. And if you're a content creator, the 2.5Gbps Ethernet port is perfect for saving high-quality VODs to a fast NAS from a streaming PC, offering the speed to stream in full quality at the same time.

If you're considering upgrading to Wi-Fi 6E, there are a few things you know before hitting the buy button. The first thing is that Wi-Fi 6E is mostly the same as Wi-Fi 6 plus access to the 6GHz spectrum, which, since it's new, has very little congestion. If you're trying to get as much speed as possible in an apartment building or condominium, this can be extremely helpful; but in a suburb or rural neighborhood, the 6 to 6E upgrade would not be worthwhile as you may see no uplift with 6GHz at all. Wi-Fi 7 routers are also becoming available, like the TP-Link Archer BE800, and can make better use of the 6GHz spectrum with wider 320MHz bands.

item.title: This cool gadget turns AI-generated images into Lego pixel art
item.itunes:title: This cool gadget turns AI-generated images into Lego pixel art
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Lego printer creates images brick by brick, using AI-generated images for limitless potential.
  • Innovative merging of Lego and AI technology automates image creation for endless fun.
  • Check out Creative Mindstorms on YouTube to see the cool Lego printer in action.

There are two kinds of Lego builders; those who like to build things for themselves, and those who like to build things that build things for themselves. And while doing either is a ton of fun, one builder has taken the latter to the extreme. Someone has created a Lego-based pritner that creates pictures made out of the bricks, plus it can accept AI-generated images for limitless potential.

2:23
Related
Google Pixel 8a review: A great mid-ranger with Google's best AI tools for half the price
Google made the Pixel 8 and Pixel 8 Pro the first real "AI smartphones," and that now extends to the $500 Pixel 8a.

A Lego printer that makes any image you want, brick by brick

This cool creation is the idea of Creative Mindstorms on YouTube. They're no strangers to AI or Lego, as they've created several videos where they've merged the two in perfect harmony. In this episode, they're creating a mix of a printer and a Lego builder to create something that makes anything you throw at it.

The idea behind the tech is that you feed the printer an image and it figures out which blocks go where. It then automatically places all of the blocks where they need to go. Of course, an AI generatedd image is going to feature a ton of colors, not all of which a Lego set can handle. As such, before anything gets fed to the printer, there's another piece of software that figures out how to translate an image into something that looks like pixel art. This can then be translated into Lego and then 'printed' out.

It's a really cool way to automate the image creation process, so if you're interested, be sure to give Creative Mindstorm's video a look above. And if this has otten you in the mood to build more stuff, why not check out our review of the KBDcraft kit and learn how you can make your own keyboard?

item.title: Ranking all the Windows Start menus, from worst to best
item.itunes:title: Ranking all the Windows Start menus, from worst to best
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Windows 8's redesign of the Start menu was a flop - ditching the button and implementing a screen full of tiles aggravated users.
  • Windows 8.1 made fixes by bringing the Start button back, but the Start screen was still a far cry from the beloved menu interface.
  • Windows 11's Start menu caused controversy with unnecessary elements like a Recommended section and ads.

Windows may be almost 39 years old now, but many of the features available in Windows 11 have their roots in the past versions of Microsoft’s flagship OS. Take the iconic Start menu, for instance. First implemented in Windows 95, it has become a stable UI element of the Windows ecosystem.

But just like the different versions of Windows, not all Start menus were built the same. While certain menus are universally adored by the Windows community, there are just as many versions of the Start menu that attracted a lot of criticism from the crowd. So, in this article, we’ll rank the Start menus included in every major version of the Windows lineup of operating systems.

4:31
Related
Evolution of the Windows Start menu: How we got to where we are today
The Start menu has been a staple of Windows for nearly 30 years. Here's how it's evolved in that time.

9 Windows 8

The black sheep of the Windows family

Innovation is always a plus in technology, but sometimes, companies tend to disregard the old adage “if it ain't broke, don't fix it” when implementing new functionalities to a well-loved product. Microsoft learned this lesson the hard way when the Washington-based firm released Windows 8.

If I were to talk about everything that was wrong with the OS, we’d be here all day. In short, Microsoft, in its pursuit of catering to both PC and tablet crowds, redesigned the Start menu from the ground up, and the final product was a Start screen rather than a menu. Ditching the Start button was a bad idea on its own, but Microsoft decided to shoot itself in the foot by implementing a tile-based menu that covered the whole screen. In the end, Windows 8 went down as one of the most reviled operating systems ever released by Microsoft (which was quite the achievement, given Windows ME’s reputation).

8 Windows 8.1

A step in the right direction, but the damage was already done

After receiving relentless criticism for Windows 8, Microsoft decided to address some of the backlash with the release of Windows 8.1. The updated version of the highly detested OS brought the Start button back to the Taskbar. Although the Start screen still wasn’t replaced with the beloved menu-like interface, it was a lot more bearable than before.

For starters, Microsoft added the Power button at the top of the screen, right next to a Search button. Besides slashing the number of tiles available on the Start screen, Windows 8.1 also added an arrow under the tiles. Clicking on it made the applications and software installed on your system visible, making it similar to the All Apps/Program options on older operating systems. Despite the improvements, the Start screen of Windows 8.1 was far from perfect, causing Microsoft to ditch the redesigned Start UI in subsequent versions.

7 Windows 11

Weird changes that nobody asked for

Being an avid lover of Windows 10, I may be biased when placing the Start menu of Windows 11 this low on the list. I’ll admit, I’m not very fond of the tiles introduced in Windows 8, and Microsoft doing away with them in Windows 11 was definitely a positive in my book. But for some reason, Microsoft decided to switch the default position of the Start button to the middle instead of the bottom left part of the screen. Luckily, you can switch its position back to where it was in Windows 10, though Windows 11 doesn’t let you move the rest of the Taskbar along with the button.

And that’s all you can do on the customization front. There’s no way to resize the Start bar without resorting to third-party apps like Start11. The new additions, like the Recommended section, are rather redundant. Rather than displaying your often-used apps, it shows your recently accessed apps and files. Plus, there’s no way to make it permanently disappear, as Windows continues showing a tiny Recommended bar at the bottom of the Start menu even after you disable it with the help of certain tweaks. Then there’s the three-lettered blight Microsoft added to the Start menu last year: ads.

6 Windows 8.1 RT

Or maybe I should say, Windows 10: Lite

Source: Microsoft

Windows RT devices are one of the many products resting in Microsoft’s graveyard. But one thing’s for sure: the Start menu added in the Windows 8.1 RT update was a breath of fresh air. Sharing several design cues with the Start menu featured Windows 10, the one featured in the 8.1 RT update discarded the Start screen for the menu-like layout we know and love.

That said, it still had some differences that set it apart from its Windows 10 cousin. For example, the Power button was pushed to the top of the Start menu instead of the lower left corner of the screen. The Settings and Documents buttons weren’t as accessible either, though it was nothing like the trainwreck of the Start screen that we saw in Windows 8.

5 Windows 95/NT 4.0/2000

A solid, compact Start menu

The fifth rank for the best Windows Start menu is a tie between Windows 95, NT 4.0, and 2000. Microsoft exposed the world to the greatness of the Start menu in the Chicago build of Windows 95. The fact that the earliest version of the Start menu sits at the middle of the list should speak volumes of how impressive it was back in the day.

Despite its limited number of buttons and icons, the Start menu on Windows 95 provided easy access to every service or application you may require on your PC. The Programs option was the precursor to the All Apps button we have on present-day Windows operating systems, and the rest of the options, including Find, Run, Help, Run, and Settings were useful in their own rights.

And yes, while Windows NT and 2000 were released years after Windows 95, their Start menus were identical to the one included in the latter. Besides featuring a banner with its name, Windows NT 4.0 got rid of the Suspend button, while Windows 2000 included a similar banner and added the Set Program Access and Defaults option to the menu.

4 Windows 98/ME

Better support for multi-user systems

Windows 98 | Image credit: Wikipedia (Paowee)

The successor to Windows 95 may not have redesigned the Start menu, but it brought some helpful little additions to the interface. The inclusion of a Log-Off button was quite useful for users who had to share their Windows machines. Similarly, the Windows Update option provided an easy way to check for any pending updates to your PC, while the Favorites folder displayed your most-used apps.

Some might call the extra options "bloatware," but they were pretty useful additions to the Start menu. The Windows ME OS, for all its faults, didn’t falter when it came to the Start menu, either. And that’s because it didn’t attempt to reinvent the wheel (or menu, in this case), unlike a certain OS ranked dead last on this list.

3 Windows XP

An improvement over its predecessors in every way

Windows XP brought several changes to the typical Windows UI and totally redesigned the Start menu for the better. Instead of featuring the old-school menu included in its predecessors, the Start menu was split into panels, a trend that continued all the way until Windows 10. The Windows 95/98/Millennium/2000/NT 4.0 banner was scrapped, with the left pane including your frequently-used apps alongside any software you pinned for easy access.

Meanwhile, the right pane housed the Documents, Control Panel, Search, and Find and Run utilities, akin to the tools included in older Windows operating systems. The only difference was that the Programs option was turned into the All Programs button and placed under the left pane, with the Log-Off and Shut Down buttons placed underneath.

While some might label Windows XP as too colorful and criticize Microsoft for cramming too many options in the Start menu, it was a landmark OS that single-handedly modernized the Windows lineup.

2 Windows 10

A culmination of the best design aspects, mixed with a certain toxin

When Windows 8 crashed and burned, Microsoft received a second chance to set everything right with Windows 10… and that’s more-or-less what the company did. The Start screen was dismantled, and the Start menu was brought back to the table Windows.

The left side of the ol’ dual-pane setup now features your recently installed apps, alongside a comprehensive list of the software on your PC and a certain unwanted component that I’ll get to in a bit. The Power, Settings, Account, and other buttons were moved to the extreme left, while the right pane now featured live tiles that you could customize and group together.

While I’m not the biggest fan of the tiles, I still would have granted the top spot to the sleek and good-looking Start menu of Windows 10, if not for a terrible addition: advertisements. Windows 11 may have gathered a lot of flak for ads in the Start menu, but it was Windows 10 where Microsoft began deploying advertisements via the Suggested section.

1 Windows Vista/7

The best iterations of the Start menu to date

Love it or hate it, Microsoft nailed the Start menu in Windows Vista. While the changes were mostly on the appearance side, the translucent Start menu looked far better than what we had on Windows XP. The Start wording was also ditched in favor of a compact Windows button.

Functionality-wise, it was largely similar to its predecessor, but with a few quality-of-life improvements. The Search button was removed, with a Search Bar occupying the spot right above the taskbar. The right pane also had more options, including one to quickly open the Games folder, while the Power and Log-Off wordings were dropped from their respective buttons.

Windows 7 featured more-or-less the same layout, though the Log-Off button was quietly moved to a drop-down menu. All-in-all, Windows Vista and Windows 7 packed the best versions of the Start menu. The reason? The Start menus were neither overly complicated nor bloated with useless features or ads.

How will the Start menu evolve from here on?

Besides the TPM requirements, the Start menu was another aspect where Windows 11 disappointed its long-term users. Heck, the very fact that a Microsoft employee agreed that the menu isn’t good enough proves that Windows 11 dropped the ball when it comes to designing a useful and practical Start menu.

But the real question is, what’s next? When Microsoft inevitably releases Windows 12, perhaps the company will introduce a new Start menu similar to the one from the Windows Vista/7 era. Or maybe, the tech giant will double down on advertisements and AI integrations nobody asked for. The answer to these questions is anybody’s guess, but I just hope that whatever Microsoft cooks up doesn’t turn out like Windows 8.

Related
Windows 12 will fail if Microsoft uses excessive AI
I'm all excited to see what new Windows 12 features Microsoft has in store for us. But the possible infusion of AI into the OS has me worried.

Windows XP brought several changes to the typical Windows UI and totally redesigned the Start menu for the better. Instead of featuring the old-school menu included in its predecessors, the Start menu was split into panels, a trend that continued all the way until Windows 10. The Windows 95/98/Millennium/2000/NT 4.0 banner was scrapped, with the left pane including your frequently-used apps alongside any software you pinned for easy access.

Meanwhile, the right pane housed the Documents, Control Panel, Search, and Find and Run utilities, akin to the tools included in older Windows operating systems. The only difference was that the Programs option was turned into the All Programs button and placed under the left pane, with the Log-Off and Shut Down buttons placed underneath.

While some might label Windows XP as too colorful and criticize Microsoft for cramming too many options in the Start menu, it was a landmark OS that single-handedly modernized the Windows lineup.

2 Windows 10

A culmination of the best design aspects, mixed with a certain toxin

When Windows 8 crashed and burned, Microsoft received a second chance to set everything right with Windows 10… and that’s more-or-less what the company did. The Start screen was dismantled, and the Start menu was brought back to the table Windows.

The left side of the ol’ dual-pane setup now features your recently installed apps, alongside a comprehensive list of the software on your PC and a certain unwanted component that I’ll get to in a bit. The Power, Settings, Account, and other buttons were moved to the extreme left, while the right pane now featured live tiles that you could customize and group together.

While I’m not the biggest fan of the tiles, I still would have granted the top spot to the sleek and good-looking Start menu of Windows 10, if not for a terrible addition: advertisements. Windows 11 may have gathered a lot of flak for ads in the Start menu, but it was Windows 10 where Microsoft began deploying advertisements via the Suggested section.

1 Windows Vista/7

The best iterations of the Start menu to date

Love it or hate it, Microsoft nailed the Start menu in Windows Vista. While the changes were mostly on the appearance side, the translucent Start menu looked far better than what we had on Windows XP. The Start wording was also ditched in favor of a compact Windows button.

Functionality-wise, it was largely similar to its predecessor, but with a few quality-of-life improvements. The Search button was removed, with a Search Bar occupying the spot right above the taskbar. The right pane also had more options, including one to quickly open the Games folder, while the Power and Log-Off wordings were dropped from their respective buttons.

Windows 7 featured more-or-less the same layout, though the Log-Off button was quietly moved to a drop-down menu. All-in-all, Windows Vista and Windows 7 packed the best versions of the Start menu. The reason? The Start menus were neither overly complicated nor bloated with useless features or ads.

How will the Start menu evolve from here on?

Besides the TPM requirements, the Start menu was another aspect where Windows 11 disappointed its long-term users. Heck, the very fact that a Microsoft employee agreed that the menu isn’t good enough proves that Windows 11 dropped the ball when it comes to designing a useful and practical Start menu.

But the real question is, what’s next? When Microsoft inevitably releases Windows 12, perhaps the company will introduce a new Start menu similar to the one from the Windows Vista/7 era. Or maybe, the tech giant will double down on advertisements and AI integrations nobody asked for. The answer to these questions is anybody’s guess, but I just hope that whatever Microsoft cooks up doesn’t turn out like Windows 8.

Related
Windows 12 will fail if Microsoft uses excessive AI
I'm all excited to see what new Windows 12 features Microsoft has in store for us. But the possible infusion of AI into the OS has me worried.
item.content:encoded:
Key Takeaways

  • Windows 8's redesign of the Start menu was a flop - ditching the button and implementing a screen full of tiles aggravated users.
  • Windows 8.1 made fixes by bringing the Start button back, but the Start screen was still a far cry from the beloved menu interface.
  • Windows 11's Start menu caused controversy with unnecessary elements like a Recommended section and ads.

Windows may be almost 39 years old now, but many of the features available in Windows 11 have their roots in the past versions of Microsoft’s flagship OS. Take the iconic Start menu, for instance. First implemented in Windows 95, it has become a stable UI element of the Windows ecosystem.

But just like the different versions of Windows, not all Start menus were built the same. While certain menus are universally adored by the Windows community, there are just as many versions of the Start menu that attracted a lot of criticism from the crowd. So, in this article, we’ll rank the Start menus included in every major version of the Windows lineup of operating systems.

4:31
Related
Evolution of the Windows Start menu: How we got to where we are today
The Start menu has been a staple of Windows for nearly 30 years. Here's how it's evolved in that time.

9 Windows 8

The black sheep of the Windows family

Innovation is always a plus in technology, but sometimes, companies tend to disregard the old adage “if it ain't broke, don't fix it” when implementing new functionalities to a well-loved product. Microsoft learned this lesson the hard way when the Washington-based firm released Windows 8.

If I were to talk about everything that was wrong with the OS, we’d be here all day. In short, Microsoft, in its pursuit of catering to both PC and tablet crowds, redesigned the Start menu from the ground up, and the final product was a Start screen rather than a menu. Ditching the Start button was a bad idea on its own, but Microsoft decided to shoot itself in the foot by implementing a tile-based menu that covered the whole screen. In the end, Windows 8 went down as one of the most reviled operating systems ever released by Microsoft (which was quite the achievement, given Windows ME’s reputation).

8 Windows 8.1

A step in the right direction, but the damage was already done

After receiving relentless criticism for Windows 8, Microsoft decided to address some of the backlash with the release of Windows 8.1. The updated version of the highly detested OS brought the Start button back to the Taskbar. Although the Start screen still wasn’t replaced with the beloved menu-like interface, it was a lot more bearable than before.

For starters, Microsoft added the Power button at the top of the screen, right next to a Search button. Besides slashing the number of tiles available on the Start screen, Windows 8.1 also added an arrow under the tiles. Clicking on it made the applications and software installed on your system visible, making it similar to the All Apps/Program options on older operating systems. Despite the improvements, the Start screen of Windows 8.1 was far from perfect, causing Microsoft to ditch the redesigned Start UI in subsequent versions.

7 Windows 11

Weird changes that nobody asked for

Being an avid lover of Windows 10, I may be biased when placing the Start menu of Windows 11 this low on the list. I’ll admit, I’m not very fond of the tiles introduced in Windows 8, and Microsoft doing away with them in Windows 11 was definitely a positive in my book. But for some reason, Microsoft decided to switch the default position of the Start button to the middle instead of the bottom left part of the screen. Luckily, you can switch its position back to where it was in Windows 10, though Windows 11 doesn’t let you move the rest of the Taskbar along with the button.

And that’s all you can do on the customization front. There’s no way to resize the Start bar without resorting to third-party apps like Start11. The new additions, like the Recommended section, are rather redundant. Rather than displaying your often-used apps, it shows your recently accessed apps and files. Plus, there’s no way to make it permanently disappear, as Windows continues showing a tiny Recommended bar at the bottom of the Start menu even after you disable it with the help of certain tweaks. Then there’s the three-lettered blight Microsoft added to the Start menu last year: ads.

6 Windows 8.1 RT

Or maybe I should say, Windows 10: Lite

Source: Microsoft

Windows RT devices are one of the many products resting in Microsoft’s graveyard. But one thing’s for sure: the Start menu added in the Windows 8.1 RT update was a breath of fresh air. Sharing several design cues with the Start menu featured Windows 10, the one featured in the 8.1 RT update discarded the Start screen for the menu-like layout we know and love.

That said, it still had some differences that set it apart from its Windows 10 cousin. For example, the Power button was pushed to the top of the Start menu instead of the lower left corner of the screen. The Settings and Documents buttons weren’t as accessible either, though it was nothing like the trainwreck of the Start screen that we saw in Windows 8.

5 Windows 95/NT 4.0/2000

A solid, compact Start menu

The fifth rank for the best Windows Start menu is a tie between Windows 95, NT 4.0, and 2000. Microsoft exposed the world to the greatness of the Start menu in the Chicago build of Windows 95. The fact that the earliest version of the Start menu sits at the middle of the list should speak volumes of how impressive it was back in the day.

Despite its limited number of buttons and icons, the Start menu on Windows 95 provided easy access to every service or application you may require on your PC. The Programs option was the precursor to the All Apps button we have on present-day Windows operating systems, and the rest of the options, including Find, Run, Help, Run, and Settings were useful in their own rights.

And yes, while Windows NT and 2000 were released years after Windows 95, their Start menus were identical to the one included in the latter. Besides featuring a banner with its name, Windows NT 4.0 got rid of the Suspend button, while Windows 2000 included a similar banner and added the Set Program Access and Defaults option to the menu.

4 Windows 98/ME

Better support for multi-user systems

Windows 98 | Image credit: Wikipedia (Paowee)

The successor to Windows 95 may not have redesigned the Start menu, but it brought some helpful little additions to the interface. The inclusion of a Log-Off button was quite useful for users who had to share their Windows machines. Similarly, the Windows Update option provided an easy way to check for any pending updates to your PC, while the Favorites folder displayed your most-used apps.

Some might call the extra options "bloatware," but they were pretty useful additions to the Start menu. The Windows ME OS, for all its faults, didn’t falter when it came to the Start menu, either. And that’s because it didn’t attempt to reinvent the wheel (or menu, in this case), unlike a certain OS ranked dead last on this list.

3 Windows XP

An improvement over its predecessors in every way

Windows XP brought several changes to the typical Windows UI and totally redesigned the Start menu for the better. Instead of featuring the old-school menu included in its predecessors, the Start menu was split into panels, a trend that continued all the way until Windows 10. The Windows 95/98/Millennium/2000/NT 4.0 banner was scrapped, with the left pane including your frequently-used apps alongside any software you pinned for easy access.

Meanwhile, the right pane housed the Documents, Control Panel, Search, and Find and Run utilities, akin to the tools included in older Windows operating systems. The only difference was that the Programs option was turned into the All Programs button and placed under the left pane, with the Log-Off and Shut Down buttons placed underneath.

While some might label Windows XP as too colorful and criticize Microsoft for cramming too many options in the Start menu, it was a landmark OS that single-handedly modernized the Windows lineup.

2 Windows 10

A culmination of the best design aspects, mixed with a certain toxin

When Windows 8 crashed and burned, Microsoft received a second chance to set everything right with Windows 10… and that’s more-or-less what the company did. The Start screen was dismantled, and the Start menu was brought back to the table Windows.

The left side of the ol’ dual-pane setup now features your recently installed apps, alongside a comprehensive list of the software on your PC and a certain unwanted component that I’ll get to in a bit. The Power, Settings, Account, and other buttons were moved to the extreme left, while the right pane now featured live tiles that you could customize and group together.

While I’m not the biggest fan of the tiles, I still would have granted the top spot to the sleek and good-looking Start menu of Windows 10, if not for a terrible addition: advertisements. Windows 11 may have gathered a lot of flak for ads in the Start menu, but it was Windows 10 where Microsoft began deploying advertisements via the Suggested section.

1 Windows Vista/7

The best iterations of the Start menu to date

Love it or hate it, Microsoft nailed the Start menu in Windows Vista. While the changes were mostly on the appearance side, the translucent Start menu looked far better than what we had on Windows XP. The Start wording was also ditched in favor of a compact Windows button.

Functionality-wise, it was largely similar to its predecessor, but with a few quality-of-life improvements. The Search button was removed, with a Search Bar occupying the spot right above the taskbar. The right pane also had more options, including one to quickly open the Games folder, while the Power and Log-Off wordings were dropped from their respective buttons.

Windows 7 featured more-or-less the same layout, though the Log-Off button was quietly moved to a drop-down menu. All-in-all, Windows Vista and Windows 7 packed the best versions of the Start menu. The reason? The Start menus were neither overly complicated nor bloated with useless features or ads.

How will the Start menu evolve from here on?

Besides the TPM requirements, the Start menu was another aspect where Windows 11 disappointed its long-term users. Heck, the very fact that a Microsoft employee agreed that the menu isn’t good enough proves that Windows 11 dropped the ball when it comes to designing a useful and practical Start menu.

But the real question is, what’s next? When Microsoft inevitably releases Windows 12, perhaps the company will introduce a new Start menu similar to the one from the Windows Vista/7 era. Or maybe, the tech giant will double down on advertisements and AI integrations nobody asked for. The answer to these questions is anybody’s guess, but I just hope that whatever Microsoft cooks up doesn’t turn out like Windows 8.

Related
Windows 12 will fail if Microsoft uses excessive AI
I'm all excited to see what new Windows 12 features Microsoft has in store for us. But the possible infusion of AI into the OS has me worried.

item.title: How to remove Microsoft Teams on Windows 11
item.itunes:title: How to remove Microsoft Teams on Windows 11
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Quick Links

  • How to remove Microsoft Teams on Windows 11
  • How to remove Microsoft Teams (for work or school) on Windows 11
  • How to remove Microsoft Teams Chat from the taskbar

Windows 11 is one of the most common operating systems used by modern PCs and laptops. While the operating system is great, the default apps on Windows aren't always the best. This is usually the first thing that users change on their OS. When you use Windows you get a slew of free applications, like Microsoft Edge, the Mail app, and even apps like Paint and even a free video editor, Clipchamp. While most of these apps are easy to uninstall or change from the default, there are more intrusive apps like Dropbox and Teams which require some deeper settings changes.

There are two versions of the Teams application that could be on your PC: a personal application and a work or school application. We are going to show you how you can uninstall them in a few easy steps.

Related
How to use Microsoft Teams on a Chromebook
Does your workplace or school use Microsoft Teams? You can get started with the app on your Chromebook in a few simple steps.

How to remove Microsoft Teams on Windows 11

We're starting first with the process of removing the Microsoft Teams app, which is the app that's pre-installed on your Windows 11 PC and pinned to the Taskbar. You'll first have to uninstall it from the Windows 11 settings app, then disable the icon in the Taskbar.

  1. Open the Windows Settings app by pressing Windows Key + I on your keyboard.
  2. Choose Apps in the sidebar.
  3. Choose Installed apps.
  4. In the search bar at the top, type in Teams.
  5. Click on the Microsoft Teams (personal) icon. The installed app is usually 250-300 MB in size. Click on the three dots next to the app.
  6. Click on Uninstall. A pop-up will appear, and you'll need to press Uninstall again.

How to remove Microsoft Teams (for work or school) on Windows 11

If you are also logged on to a Microsoft Teams work or school account, then you will have a second Teams application in the installed apps menu. So if you haven't already, follow steps 1-4 in the previous section and then proceed to the next 2 steps.

  1. To start, follow steps 1-4 in the previous section. On the Installed Apps menu look for the Teams (work or school) application, usually 120-150MB, and click on the three dots on the right.
  2. Then select Uninstall. A popup will appear, and you'll have to click Uninstall again.

How to remove Microsoft Teams Chat from the taskbar

  1. If you still have a Teams icon on your taskbar or system tray, you must turn off Chats. To do this, right-click on the Taskbar and navigate to your Taskbar settings.
  2. Turn the icon for Chat to off.

That's it! Your PC is now free of Microsoft Teams chat. You will have gained back some space on your Windows install and no longer have the Chat icon on your Taskbar. Sometimes, this app might reinstall itself after a featured Windows 11 update like the Moment 3 Update, but you can also go back and disable it.

Less bloatware and more space

You now have Microsoft Teams removed from your Windows PC, sparing you some space, as well as an extra spot on your Taskbar with Chat removed. Check out our other Windows 11 guides, like the option to enable the seconds on the Windows 11 clock and how to use Android apps on Windows 11 if you want to further customize your default Windows 11 experience.
